title: Paper Title
content: Ultrasonic spray pyrolysis synthesis of Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalysts for simultaneous H2 production and CO2 reduction

title: Abstract
content: Simultaneous photocatalytic hydrogen production and CO 2 reduction (to form CO and CH 4 ) from water using methanol as a hole scavenger were investigated using silver-modified TiO 2 (AgTiO 2 ) nanocomposite catalysts.A simple ultrasonic spray pyrolysis (SP) method was used to prepare mesoporous AgTiO 2 composite particles using TiO 2 (P25) and AgNO 3 as the precursors.The material properties and photocatalytic activities were compared with those prepared by a conventional wet-impregnation (WI) method.It was found that the samples prepared by the SP method had a larger specific surface area and a better dispersion of Ag nanoparticles on TiO 2 than those prepared by the WI method, and as a result, the SP samples showed much higher photocatalytic activities toward H 2 production and CO 2 reduction.The optimal Ag concentration on TiO 2 was found to be 2 wt.The H 2 production rate of the 2 AgTiO 2 eSP sample exhibited a six-fold enhancement compared with the 2 AgTiO 2 eWI sample and a sixty-fold enhancement compared with bare TiO 2 .The molar ratio of H 2 and CO in the final products can be tuned in the range from 2 to 10 by varying the reaction gas composition, suggesting a viable way of producing syngas (a mixture of H 2 and CO) from CO 2 and water using the prepared AgTiO 2 catalysts with energy input from the sun.

title: Experimental
content: The apparatus for ultrasonic spray pyrolysis process and the formation mechanism of AgTiO 2 nanoparticle composites are shown in Fig. 2. The spraying solution was prepared by mixing aqueous AgNO 3 solution and TiO 2 (Degussa P25) nanoparticles and sonicating for 20 min.The TiO 2 concentration was fixed at 16 mgml and the amount of AgNO 3 was varied so that the nominal weight percentage of Ag to TiO 2 in the catalysts was in the range of 1e8.The ultrasonic nebulizer (Sonaer) has a frequency of 2.4 MHz and produces droplets with a volumetric mean diameter of 1.8 mm.The generated fine mists containing AgNO 3 TiO 2 were carried out by nitrogen and passed through a diffusion dryer composed of silica gels where the solvent (i.e., water) evaporated and AgNO 3 concentrated on the surface of TiO 2 particles.The particles then passed through a cylindrical quartz reactor (L A D 14 37 A 2.2 cm) that was placed inside a tube furnace (Thermo Fisher) with a set temperature of 550 C. The flow rate of carrier gas was 2.0 lmin which corresponded to a residence time of 4 s in the heating zone.The AgNO 3 was thermally decomposed and silver ions were reduced to metallic silver and deposited on the surface of TiO 2 forming AgTiO 2 composites.The spray-pyrolyzed particles were collected on a glass fiber filter placed inside a stainless-steel filter holder.Pure TiO 2 sample was also prepared using the same method without addition of AgNO 3 .As a control, Ag modified TiO 2 samples with the same nominal Ag weight percentages were also synthesized by a benchmark wet-impregnation method by mixing and stirring desired amount of TiO 2 (P25) in AgNO 3 aqueous solution at 80 C overnight and finally calcining the sample in air environment at 400 C for 4 h.To have a comparable basis for material property, the spray pyrolysis samples were also calcined in air at 400 C for 4 h as a final step.The samples were denoted as mAgTiO 2 eSP or mAg TiO 2 eWI, where m represents the nominal Ag weight percentage, SP represents materials prepared by spray pyrolysis method, and WI represents materials prepared by wetimpregnation method. The BrunauereEmmetteTeller (BET) specific surface area of the catalysts was measured by nitrogen adsorption using a surface area and porosity analyzer (Micrometrics ASAP 2020).The crystal structures of the catalysts were identified by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Scintag XDS 2000) using Cu Ka irradiation at 45 kV and a diffracted beam monochromator operated at 40 mA in the 2q range from 20 to 80 at a scan rate of 2 min.The UVevis reflectance spectra were recorded with a UVevis spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics) using BaSO 4 as the background.The reflectance was converted to F(R) using the KubelkaeMunk (KeM) function F(R) 14 (1AR) 2 2R, and the band gap energy was obtained from the plot of F(R)E ph 12 against the photon energy E ph 16.Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Hitachi S4800) was used to analyze the catalyst morphology and surface elemental composition. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H9000NAR) and high-resolution TEM were used to examine the particle size and dispersion of Ag nanoparticles on TiO 2 as well as the lattice information. The schematic of the photocatalytic reaction system is illustrated in Fig. 3. Compressed N 2 (99.998,Praxair) or CO 2 (99.999,Praxair) was used as the carrier gas for measuring the catalytic activity of H 2 production or CO 2 reduction, respectively.The carrier gas regulated by a mass flow controller passed through a bubbler containing 5 vol methanol in de-ionized water to generate a wateremethanol vapor mixture.Methanol was expected to serve as the hole scavenger and thus enhance the rate of reduction by electrons 8,13,31.Estimated from the saturation vapor pressureetemperature profile 32, the volume fraction of H 2 O and CH 3 OH in the gas mixture at 20 C was calculated to be 2.2 and 0.6, respectively, balanced with the carrier gas (N 2 or CO 2 ).The gas mixture was then introduced into a cylindrical photoreactor with stainless steel walls and a quartz window.100 mg powder catalysts were dispersed on a glass fiber filter and placed at the bottom of the reactor.A 150 W Solar Simulator (Oriel) was used as the photo-excitation source.The light intensity, measured by a spectroradiometer (International Light Technologies ILT950), was 12.5 mWcm 2 for 200 l 400 nm and 69.6 mWcm 2 for 200 l 1000 nm.Before photo-illumination, the reactor was purged with the carrier gas (N 2 or CO 2 ) at a flow rate of 120 mlmin for 1 h and then at 4 ml min for 1 h.During the illumination, the carrier gas flow rate maintained at 4 mlmin (i.e., the reactor was operated as a continuous-flow mode), and the sample of effluent gas was taken every 30 min by an automated valve to a gas chromatograph (GC, Agilent 7890A).The GC was equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD, for measurement of H 2 and CO) and flame ionization detector (FID, for measurement of CH 4 and other organic compounds).H 2 and CO were measured in separate experiments by using different GC carrier gases for the TCD.Due to the close thermal conductivity of H 2 and He, N 2 was used as GC carrier gas for H 2 measurement, while He was used as GC carrier gas for CO measurement.

title: Results and discussion
content: 3.1.Crystal structure Ag concentration (i.e., 2), no diffraction lines of Ag species were observed, suggesting that Ag is well dispersed or the particle size is very small.At a higher Ag concentration (i.e., 8), the SP sample showed peaks at 2q 14 38.2 , 44.4 , 64.5 and 77.4 that correspond to metallic silver 8,30,33,34 and no characteristic peaks of silver oxides were observed.This indicates that Ag ions are thermally reduced during the spray pyrolysis process 35.In contrast, for the 8 AgTiO 2 eWI sample, neither metallic silver or silver oxides were observed.The result implies that silver oxides could be the major Ag species on WI samples as some literature publications reported the presence of Ag 2 O in a AgTiO 2 composite prepared by impregnation 26 without detecting Ag 2 O diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns.This can be further confirmed in the TEM analysis described in a later section in the paper. The textural properties of the AgTiO 2 samples were characterized by BET, SEMEDX, and TEMHRTEM analysis.Table 1 compares the results of BET analysis for the SP and WI samples with the same nominal Ag concentration (i.e., 2). The SP sample had a larger specific surface area (54.2 m 2 g) compared with the WI sample (43.8 m 2 g).The pore volume and average pore size for the SP sample were also larger than those of the WI sample.The results suggest that the spray pyrolysis process may lessen the degree of agglomeration of the TiO 2 nanoparticles through the solvent evaporation process, resulting in a larger pore size and a larger surface area.The pore size in the range of 15e18 nm indicate a mesoporous structure for both samples, which is confirmed by the SEM images shown in Fig. 5.The SP samples (Fig. 5a) are mesoporous spheres composed of the primary nanoparticles while the WI samples (Fig. 5b) are irregular-shape agglomerates of the primary nanoparticles.By counting more than a hundred of spheres of the SP sample, the particle size distribution was obtained and shown in Fig. 5c.The particles range in the size of 0.2e2.4mm, with a mean size of 0.9 mm and a mode at 0.7 mm.TEM and HRTEM images for the SP and WI samples are shown in Fig. 6.It is clear that Ag nanoparticles in the size range of 1e2 nm are well dispersed on crystalline TiO 2 particles (w20 nm, agreeing with P25 primary particle size) for both SP and WI samples.However, some "big" Ag nanoparticles (w8 nm, see Fig. 6d) and aggregates of Ag nanoparticles (see Fig. 6e) were found on the WI sample, indicating that the Ag dispersion on the WI sample was worse than on the SP sample.The EDX elemental analysis data for the 2 AgTiO 2 samples are listed in Table 1.A major difference between the SP and WI samples is the measured Ag concentration, although both of them had the same nominal Ag concentration (i.e., 2 wt) calculated from the precursor composition in the starting solution.For the WI sample, the measured Ag concentration (2.2 wt) was very close to the nominal concentration, while for the SP sample, the measured Ag concentration was only 1.2 wt.A possible reason for the lower Ag concentration on SP samples could be that a portion of the Ag nanoparticles formed during the spray pyrolysis escaped from the TiO 2 surface along with the solvent evaporation process and these Ag nanoparticles were not collected on the filter due to their extremely small size. Band gap analysis Fig. 7 shows the results of band gap analysis derived from the UVevis diffuse reflectance spectra.Pure TiO 2 P25 has a band gap of 3.20 eV.With incorporation of Ag species, the band gap slightly decreased to 3.0e3.1 eV for SP samples and 2.9e3.0 eV for WI samples.However, the change in band gap is not an apparent function of Ag concentration.The shift of band gap is probably due to the interaction of Ag ions with TiO 2 26 or formation of impurity band inside the TiO 2 band gap 36,37.A broad peak around 2.35 eV (or 500e550 nm in absorption spectra) was observed for both SP and WI samples with higher Ag concentration (4 wt).This unique peak is an indication of the localized surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of Ag nanoparticles 26,38, which again confirms the presence of metallic Ag on TiO 2 .The intensities of SPR peaks on SP samples are higher than those on WI samples, confirming that SP samples have more metallic Ag, which is in agreement with the results from XRD and TEM analysis. The experiments of photocatalytic hydrogen production were carried out by using N 2 as the carrier gas to bubble through the methanolwater mixture.Since the reactor was running in a continuous-flow mode, the H 2 production rate gradually increased and reached a steady value after approximately 4 h of photo-illumination (the curves of H 2 production rate with illumination time are not shown here).Fig. 8 summarizes the steady-state H 2 production rate for SP and WI samples with different Ag concentrations.For bare TiO 2 , the H 2 production rate was very small for both SP and WI samples.The incorporation of Ag species significantly enhanced the H 2 production rate.For AgTiO 2 samples with Ag concentration greater than 1, the SP samples were much more active than the WI samples and the optimal Ag concentration appeared to be 2.For 2 AgTiO 2 eSP, the H 2 production rate was 1130 mmolgh, 6 times as much as that of 2 AgTiO 2 eWI (180 mmolgh), and 60 times as much as that of bare TiO 2 eSP (19 mmolgh).Ag concentration greater than 2 resulted in a lower H 2 production rate, probably because too high a metal concentration on TiO 2 leads to the formation of electron-hole recombination centers 15.The H 2 production rate of the 2 AgTiO 2 eSP was compared with the literature data.Park et al. 8 reported that the H 2 production rate was 8560 mmolgh by a AgTiO 2 (rutile) powder catalyst suspended in a methanolwater (1:1 vv) solution under 365 nm UV irradiation at an intensity of 36 W cm 2 .The relatively high yield was attributed to the extremely high light intensity.Alenzi N. et al. 31 reported the average H 2 production rate of a AgTiO 2 (anatase) film was 148 mmolh g in a methanolwater (1:1 vv) solution system (4.7 mmolhg if without Ag) under UV irradiation at 10 mWcm 2 .Onsuratoom et al. 13 reported the highest H 2 production rate was 4 cm 3 g h or 178 mmolgh on an optimized AgTiO 2 eZrO 2 photocatalyst (Ag 14 0.5 wt) in a methanolwater (1:1 vv) aqueous system under UV light at 2.3 mWcm 2 .In this work, the H 2 production rate of 2 AgTiO 2 eSP was 1130 mmolgh, with a comparable light intensity of 12.5 mWcm 2 (for 200 l 400 nm) and a much lower methanolwater ratio (0.6:2.2 vv).Comparisons with the literature data indicate that the 2 AgTiO 2 eSP catalyst we synthesized is a very effective photocatalyst toward H 2 production. The experiments of photocatalytic CO 2 reduction were carried out by using CO 2 as the carrier gas to bubble through the methanolwater mixture.Fig. 9 shows the photocatalytic activity of CO 2 reduction with the same sets of catalysts used in the H 2 production tests.CO was found to be the major product with minor CH 4 production.Like the trend observed for H 2 production, the incorporation of Ag on TiO 2 significantly increased the CO 2 reduction rate and the SP samples generally had higher activity than WI samples.The production rates of both CO and CH 4 were also affected by the Ag concentration. As shown in Fig. 9a 2 AgTiO 2 eSP demonstrates the highest CO production rate at 87 mmolhg, 50 more than that of 2 AgTiO 2 eWI (57 mmolhg).As shown in Fig. 9b, the production rates of CH 4 are one order of magnitude lower than those of CO.AgTiO 2 eSP samples with a silver concentration in the range of 2e8 had the highest and very close CH 4 production rates at approximately 10 mmolhg, more than twice as much as the average rates of WI samples in the range of 2e8 Ag.The production rate of CO was generally one order of magnitude higher than that of CH 4 , as shown in Fig. 9.The selectivity of CO and CH 4 from CO 2 photoreduction, however, has not been well understood in the literature.It has been reported in our previous work 15 that pure TiO 2 favors CO production from CO 2 photoreduction with water vapor, while the selectivity of CH 4 increases if copper species are deposited on TiO 2 surface.The results in this work on Ag-deposited TiO 2 demonstrate the same trend that Ag species promote CH 4 production rate.However, CO is the major product (90 selectivity) in both this work and our previous work 15.The reasons that the production rate of CH 4 is much smaller than CO may be: (1) it is more difficult to proceed an eight-electron transfer to generate CH 4 compared with a two-electron process for CO, and (2) CH 4 may be mainly derived from further reduction of CO and thus CH 4 formation rate is limited by that of CO.The deposited metal species on TiO 2 facilitate electron trapping and transport to the catalyst surface, and thus, can potentially enhance multi-electron transfer processes, leading to a higher rate of CH 4 . The CO 2 reduction results again confirm that SP samples are advantageous over WI samples and 2 AgTiO 2 eSP seems to have the highest overall activity in terms of both H 2 production and CO 2 reduction.By correlating to the material property data, the higher activities of SP samples compared with WI samples are mainly ascribed to the larger specific surface area (Table 1) and better dispersion of the Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 6).Another possible reason is that the SP samples have a larger fraction of metallic Ag than WI samples have as evidenced in Figs. 4 and7.It is well known that the Schottky barrier at the metal-semiconductor (AgeTiO 2 ) interface facilitates electron transfer from the semiconductor to the metal, and consequently enhancing electron-hole separation and improving photocatalytic activity.It should be noted that the actual among of Ag on SP samples was only approximately half of that on WI samples (Table 1).Hence, it is reasonable to also compare the activity of 2 AgTiO 2 eSP with 1 AgTiO 2 eWI where the two samples have similar actual Ag concentration (w1).Again, the 2 AgTiO 2 eSP sample is much more active than 1 AgTiO 2 eWI with regard to both H 2 production and CO 2 reduction. As described in the Experimental Section that it is not accurate to measure both H 2 and CO in a single photo-activity experiment because of the very low sensitivity of H 2 measurement when He was used as the GC carrier gas to measure CO.Hence, separate experiments were conducted (by using N 2 as the GC carrier gas) to measure the potential H 2 production by the same catalyst 2 AgTiO 2 eSP using a reaction gas mixture of CO 2 H 2 OCH 3 OH.By combining the two sets of experiments of H 2 and CO measurements, the feasibility of syngas (H 2 CO) production can be evaluated.The CO 2 partial pressure in the reaction gas was varied from 0 to nearly 100 to examine the potential influence on H 2 CO ratio in the products.The experimental results are summarized in Table 2, and related chemical reactions involved in the photocatalytic syngas production are listed in Reactions (1)e( 6). As shown in Table 2, for Case 1 when H 2 O CH 3 OH was the reactant gas in the absence of CO 2 , the majority of final products is H 2 (rate 14 1130 mmolhg; selectivity 14 98.3), with minor CO (rate 14 18 mmolhg; selectivity 14 1.6) and negligible CH 4 .The result indicates that direct decomposition of CH 3 OH to H 2 and CO is not likely because the CO selectivity was far less than the stoichiometry where COH 2 should be 1:2 for complete decomposition.The minor CO produced may be from multi-step decomposition process where protons are released in each step while photo-induced holes are scavenged (Reaction (3)).In Cases 2 to 4, CO 2 H 2 O CH 3 OH mixture were used as the reactant gas where the CO 2 partial pressure varied at three levels (10, 50 and 97.2), while the H 2 O and CH 3 OH partial pressure maintained constant at 2.2 and 0.6, respectively.Compared to Case 1, the production rate of H 2 in the presence of CO 2 (Cases 2e4) decreased while that of CO significantly increased and that of CH 4 remained negligible.The result clearly indicates that H 2 production (Reaction (4)) and CO 2 reduction (Reactions (5 and 6)) are competing with each other for consumption of electrons and protons, in agreement with the scheme plotted in Fig. 1.This competition mechanism is also supported by the result that a higher CO 2 partial pressure resulted in a higher CO production rate but a lower H 2 production rate (Cases 2e4).Accordingly, the H 2 CO ratio in the products varied from about 10 to 2, indicating that the ratio is controllable by varying the CO 2 partial pressure or the CO 2 H 2 O ratio in the reactants.Another way to control the H 2 CO ratio is to vary the concentration of hole scavenger, CH 3 OH, in the reaction gas.By doubling the CH 3 OH volume fraction from 0.6 to 1.2 while maintaining the same volume fractions of CO 2 and H 2 O (comparing Cases 4 It should be noted that the produced CO was mainly derived from CO 2 (Reaction (5)) and not from CH 3 OH (Reaction (3)), which is supported by the following evidences.First, in the absence of CO 2 , the selectivity of CO production is only 1.6 (Case 1), indicating that generation of CO from direct decomposition of CH 3 OH is insignificant.Second, in the presence of CO 2 , the selectivity of CO production proportionally increased with the CO 2 concentration in the reaction gas (Cases 2e4), indicating that CO production is directly correlated with CO 2 .Third, at the same CO 2 concentration, increasing the CH 3 OH concentration enhanced H 2 production rate but the CO production rate remained at the same level (Cases 4 and 5).This suggests that CO production rate is limited by CO 2 and not by CH 3 OH.If CO were derived from CH 3 OH, an increased CH 3 OH concentration could have led to a higher CO production rate.All the above experimental evidences strongly indicate that CO is indeed a product from CO 2 and not from the hole scavenger, CH 3 OH.The production of H 2 , however, could come from both H 2 O and CH 3 OH. There are very limited publications in the literature on CO 2 photoreduction by AgTiO 2 catalysts.Koci et al. 24 prepared Ag-doped TiO 2 catalysts by a sol-gel method and tested their activity for CO 2 photoreduction with water in an aqueous system under 254 nm UV irradiation.H 2 , CH 4 , CH 3 OH and CO were found to be the major products with a production rate of 6.3, 0.4, 0.07, and 0.02 mmolhg, respectively during a 24 h testing period.Those production rates were more than two orders of magnitude lower than what we have obtained in this work (for example, 220 and 103 mmolhg for H 2 and CO, respectively, as shown in Case 4 in Table 2).More importantly, the controllable ratio of H 2 CO (in the range from 2 to 10) in the reaction products and negligible CH 4 production indicates that it is a promising technology to produce syngas through photocatalytic CO 2 reduction on AgTiO 2 eSP samples prepared in this work.

title: Conclusions
content: This work has demonstrated the feasibility of syngas (H 2 and CO) production from a gas mixture of CO 2 , H 2 O and CH 3 OH through a photocatalytic reduction process on AgTiO 2 nanocomposite catalysts under solar irradiation.Although the H 2 production and CO 2 reduction processes compete for electrons and protons in the photocatalytic process, the final product selectivity (H 2 CO ratio as low as 2, with negligible CH 4 ) is in the appropriate range for subsequent Fischere-Tropsch process for liquid hydrocarbon fuel production.The selectivity is tunable by varying the reaction gas composition and the H 2 CO ratio in the range from 2 to 10 has been achieved.In addition, the material property analysis and photocatalytic activity results showed that the ultrasonic spray pyrolysis method is much superior to conventional wetimpregnation process with the advantages of smaller Ag nanoparticles, a better Ag dispersion on TiO 2 , and a higher fraction of metallic Ag species, which facilitate charge transfer and improve photocatalytic activity.The rates of photocatalytic conversion in this work were among the highest ones reported in the literature. Fig.1e Mechanism of photocatalytic hydrogen production and CO 2 reduction with water on TiO 2 semiconductor and their reduction potentials at pH 7 vs.NHE. Fig. 2 e Schematic diagram of the ultrasonic spray pyrolysis process and the formation of AgTiO 2 nanoparticle composites. Fig.3e Experimental system for photocatalytic H 2 production andor CO 2 reduction. Fig.4compares the XRD patterns of AgTiO 2 eSP and AgTiO 2 eWI samples.All patterns indicate a mixture of TiO 2 anatase and rutile phases, which agrees with the composition of P25 (approximately 80 anatase and 20 rutile).At a lower Ag concentration (i.e., 2), no diffraction lines of Ag species were observed, suggesting that Ag is well dispersed or the particle size is very small.At a higher Ag concentration (i.e., 8), the SP sample showed peaks at 2q 14 38.2 , 44.4 , 64.5 and 77.4 that correspond to metallic silver8,30,33,34 and no characteristic peaks of silver oxides were observed.This indicates that Ag ions are thermally reduced during the spray pyrolysis process35.In contrast, for the 8 AgTiO 2 eWI sample, neither metallic silver or silver oxides were observed.The result implies that silver oxides could be the major Ag species on WI samples as some literature publications reported the presence of Ag 2 O in a AgTiO 2 composite prepared by impregnation26 without detecting Ag 2 O diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns.This can be further confirmed in the TEM analysis described in a later section in the paper. Fig. 6 e TEM and HRTEM images of 2 AgTiO 2 eSP (a, b, c) and 2 AgTiO 2 eWI (d, e, f). r n a t i o n a l j o u r n a l o f h y d r o g e n e n e r g y 3 7 ( 2 0 1 2 ) 9 9 6 7 e9 9 7 6 Fig. 7 e Band gap analysis of the TiO 2 and AgTiO 2 samples prepared by ultrasonic spray pyrolysis (a) and wetimpregnation (b) methods. Fig. 8 e Rates of photocatalytic H 2 production for AgTiO 2 eSP and AgTiO 2 eWI samples at different Ag concentration (the reaction gas was a mixture of 2.2 H 2 O and 0.6 CH 3 OH balanced with N 2 ). Comparison of BET and EDX analysis results for 2 AgTiO 2 samples prepared by SP and WI methods. Production rates and product selectivity of the 2 AgTiO 2 eSP sample under simulated solar illumination.5), the H 2 CO ratio also doubled from 2.1 to 4.3, due to the reason that more protons are made available by CH 3 OH to favor H 2 production. a The reactant gas was balanced with N 2 .Fig. 9 e Rates of photocatalytic CO 2 reduction to CO (a) and CH 4 (b) for AgTiO 2 eSP and AgTiO 2 eWI samples at different Ag concentration (the reaction gas was a mixture of 97.2 CO 2 , 2.2 H 2 O and 0.6 CH 3 OH).and i n t e r n a t i o n a l j o u r n a l o f h y d r o g e n e n e r g y 3 7 ( 2 0 1 2 ) 9 9 6 7 e9 9 7 6 The authors are thankful for the support from University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee (UWM) Research Foundation Catalyst Program and from American Chemical Society e Petroleum Research Fund (ACS-PRF, Grant 50631-DNI10).Andy Krall acknowledges the Support Undergraduate Research Fellow (SURF) program in the College of Engineering and Applied Science (CEAS) at UWM. r e f e r e n c e s
